nameMeanings.name: Theobald
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: German and English: from a personal name composed of the ancient Germanic elements theud ‘people, race’ + bald ‘bold, brave’. The English surname represents a learned form, re-created from French Théobald. The usual medieval forms of the name in English and German were Tebald and Tibalt (Old French Teobaud, Tibaut). Compare Dewald and Diebold .

variants.name: Dewald
meanings: German: from the personal name Diebold, from Middle High German Dietbold, from Old High German diot ‘people, nation’ + bald ‘brave, strong’. As an American surname this is also an altered form of the cognate Theobald . Compare Davolt , Dawalt , Daywalt , Devault 3, and Dewalt 1.

History: One of the first Dewalds in North America was Johannes Michael Dewald from Medard, a village in the Palatinate, Germany, who arrived at Philadelphia, PA, in 1738 on the ship Glasgow. He married Maria Barbara Schmidt and settled in Berks County, PA. His father's surname was Theobald. Michael's descendants also bear some other altered forms of the surname, including Dewalt, Dawalt, and Davolt. — One of the earliest immigrants who bore the original German surname Dewald was Henry alias He(i)nrich Dewald from the Palatinate, Germany, who arrived in PA in 1766. His descendants mainly bear altered forms of the surname, especially Devault .

variants.name: Tidball
meanings: English (Somerset and Devon): variant of   Theobald  .

variants.name: Tebbs
meanings: English (Leicestershire and Northamptonshire): variant of Tebb, which is usually from a pet form of the Middle English male personal name Tebbold (see Theobald ).

variants.name: Tipple
meanings: English (Norfolk): from the medieval personal name Tebald, Tibalt (see Theobald ).
German: nickname for a simple person, from Low German tippel ‘point, corner’ (a reference to the pointed shape of a fool's cap).
German: from a pet form of an ancient Germanic personal name, a pet form of   Dietrich  .

variants.name: Tibbs
meanings: English: variant of Tibb, from the Middle English personal name Tibbe, which was used in the 13th century as both male (from Tibald, see Theobald ) and female name (a shortened form of Tibot, a pet form of Isabel, see Tebbutt ).

nameMeanings.name: Judith
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Biblical name, meaning ‘Jewess’ or ‘woman from Judea’, borne by a Jewish heroine whose story is recorded in the Book of Judith in the Apocrypha. Judith is portrayed as a beautiful widow who delivers her people from the invading Assyrians by gaining the confidence of their commander, Holofernes, and cutting off his head while he is asleep; without their commander, the Assyrians are duly routed. This has been a perennially popular Jewish name. In the English-speaking world it was taken up in the 16th century, having been in occasional use among Gentiles before this: for example, it was borne by a niece of William the Conqueror. It enjoyed great popularity between the 1940s and the 1960s. Today's notable bearers include the American novelist  Judith Krantz  (  b. 1928 ) and the Scottish composer  Judith Weir  (  b. 1954 ).

en.lifeSummary: When Judith Theobald was born in 1530, in England, United Kingdom, her father, Thomas Theobald, was 20 and her mother, Lady, Ann Elisy, was 18. She married James Cromwell in 1523, in England.
